Somewhere behind him on the flag deck, the Warlord's staff was already scheduling his patrol lanes, and the smelter moons came up over Ferrus like two coins paid into a very old account, and Judge Vargo — tribunal judge, turned raider, at last and forever a jurisdiction of one — went out to serve the summons.
Cask calibrated his scales in public, four times a year, and that was the whole of his religion, his politics, and his advertising.

Calibration day at the Ludo depot ran like a small rough festival: certified masses out of their case, wax seals shown around, any crew in dock invited to watch the needles, any child present allowed to set the hundred-gram mass on the pan, because a child who has watched a scale be honest grows up expecting it of scales and of men, and is usefully angry all her life when either lies.
Then Cask signed the calibration sheet, posted it at the pumps beside the price — one price, the same number to every flag, painted large — and stood the first round at the depot canteen.
A fuel baron, said the lane.
A baron takes a cut of what passes; Cask sold measured tons at posted prices and cut nobody.
The word wanted reweighing.

He had the trade from his mother, who ran a one-pump bunker station off the Ludo shadow, died owed by half the lane and owing nobody, and raised him on the catechism he still recited at calibrations: *the scale does not care who is reading it.
That is not the scale's weakness.
That is the scale's entire authority.*

Fuel was the honest commodity — grain can be watered, alloys re-stamped, news improved, but a ton of reaction mass either moves your hull or it does not, and the customer finds out exactly how much you sold him at the worst possible moment to have been lied to.
A man who cheats on fuel is not a thief.
He is a murderer on layaway.
He had put three skimming bunkermen off his docks in his life, wordlessly, with their tools, and the lane understood the silence: his scales weighed true, all the way down.
Which is why the audit of his own house, that autumn, cost him more than the blockade did.

The Halcyon-spur ledger came back with a stink in it: his bosun at the cold-spur bunker had run a rigged Wheel on the wintering gangs, taken ration chits off dorm children, and priced a card cheat's fingers at an insult rate, until Halcyon's deck chief bought the debt with a marker against Cask's own fuel ledger.
Cask went up the spur himself and did the arithmetic in front of witnesses, because correction, like calibration, is worthless done in private.
The Wheel went into the smelter.
The insult rate was voided — insult is not a mass; his scales had no pan for it.

The bosun he did not put off the dock: he broke him to hose-handler under his own former subordinates, pay docked until the dorm was made whole to the last chit, because exile teaches nothing and repayment teaches in public, gram by gram.
Cole's marker he kept live at the honest arithmetic, flight-hours against tons, and wrote to the deck chief one line: *your rate stands; my bosun's did not; the scales apologize.*
A man answered for his own pans.
All the way down.
Thorne's blockade found his depots the way frost finds pipe joints: methodically, at the weak points, in the dark.
First the reclassification: bunker fuel entered on the interdiction schedules as war materiel, making every neutral ton a citation waiting for a hull to happen to.
Then the pickets, posted off his approaches precisely where inbound tenders ran fuel-poor and slow.
Then the mines across the Ludo shadow's cheap crossing, so the honest short route priced itself out of existence and every delivery burned a margin of its own cargo going the long way round.
No depot was fired on.
Nothing so crude.

The geometry tightened, quarter by quarter, until Cask watched his gauges the way a man watches a tourniquet, and the depots dimmed one by one up the spur — not destroyed, *strangled*: a blockade, Thorne-fashion, is a proof, and a burned depot proves nothing, but a mined approach proves the route was never yours.
The architect opened a channel to him once, because architects enjoy an audience.
"Depot master.
You sell reaction mass to belligerent hulls at a posted price, without discrimination of flag.
On my schedules that is not neutrality; it is logistics for hire, and logistics is a combatant.
Close the outer depots and I log you as commerce.
Keep them open and I log you as infrastructure.
Infrastructure, master Cask, is a *target category*."
"You've weighed me, then," Cask said.
"Now weigh this.

Your cruisers bunker somewhere too, architect, and when this war ends — it will; they end; even yours — every party to it will need the one commodity nobody can re-stamp: a scale the other side also trusts.
Burn my price boards and you burn the last number in the lanes both fleets believe.
That's not a threat.
I don't stock threats.
It's a reading."
Thorne logged him as infrastructure.
Cask posted the log at the pumps under a sign in his own hand: TARGET CATEGORY, ONE PRICE.

The crews came anyway, and he hated the man with a fixed, load-rated hatred, one strangled depot at a time, the way you hate rust: not for malice, which rust lacks, but for what it costs the structure per quarter.
Mercer came in on the squeeze's worst week, hat in hand, which for Mercer meant the hat was merely present.
They had known each other thirty years, convoy master and depot master, disagreeing fluently the whole way: convoys are worthless without bunkers; bunkers are stranded capital without convoys.
Mercer wanted priority allocation, wartime rates, the whole ugly menu.
Cask poured him the good stuff and told him the price of everything on the menu was no.
"The floor price holds," Cask said.
"One number, every flag, both of us.
You lift my tons on your hulls where the mines have priced out my tenders; I bunker your convoys at the posted price.
No premium — not for war, not for friendship.
The day I charge you a war rate, Thorne's won without firing: he'll have proved the number bends, and a number that bends belongs to whoever squeezes hardest, which is him, and behind him, Kaine.
The number holding *is* the war, Mercer.
Everything else is just where the shooting happens to be."

Mercer looked at him a long time, put his glass down, and said, "You're a bad businessman, Cask," in the exact tone other men use for oaths, and they shook on it, ally to ally, and the fuel moved on convoy hulls all winter, one price, under a target category.
Ibex came at night, of course.
The unforgiven arrive after hours.

He came to buy propellant with schism metal, and the depot canteen emptied around him the way a pan empties when you lift it — four convoy dead followed that man into every room now, and would forever.

Cask weighed the metal, found it honest, sold at the posted price, and stood him the round himself in front of the empty room, because the posted price meant every flag or nothing, a friend was not an endorsement, and he had known Ibex since they were engine-deck boys under the Ferrus stacks, when the arithmetic was still a gift and not yet a doctrine.
They drank without hurry.
Then Cask gave him the reading, one friend's full measure, level to the line: "Your sums were right, Ibex.
The guild does fund both ends of the noose; I've weighed our own hypocrisy to the gram, and it's heavy.
And you spent four of the family to save your war chest, and no cause that ever came to my counter weighed what those four weighed.
I'll sell to you at the price on the board.
I'll drink with you when nobody else will.
And I will tell you, every time you dock here, that you did it, that the number was four, and that it does not average out.
That's what I have on tap for you, old friend.
Fuel at the posted price, and the true reading.
Take both or neither."

Ibex took both, and came back each quarter, which Cask understood better than the emptied room did: a man building a cause on arithmetic cannot stay away from the one counter where the arithmetic is done honestly — even when, especially when, the reading never changes.
The depots dimmed.
The price held.

On the last calibration day the crews came in past the mines to watch a child put a hundred grams on the pan under a sign that said TARGET CATEGORY, and Cask signed the sheet and posted the number, one number, every flag, and stood his round.
The scale does not care who is reading it.
That was not its weakness.
Rule one of the medicine trade: you do not count the doses until they are in an arm.

Count them in the hold and you will lose some — to heat, to a checkpoint, to the eleven small gods of spoilage — and every one you counted will cost you double, because now it has a number, and numbered things have ghosts.
Fenn counted crates.
Crates were furniture.
Nobody ever flew worse for grieving furniture.
Rule two: medicine wants to move.
This was not superstition, whatever the dock said about Fenn and his rules; it was thermodynamics with a bedside manner.

Every vial in his hold was a clock — antivirals ticking toward expiry, dermal cultures living on borrowed cold, fever kits worth everything the week before the fever crested and nothing after — and a checkpoint was a place where clocks went to be weighed, slowly, by people paid to be slow.
The Synod had discovered you could conquer a world without boarding it: close the lane, weigh the cargo, let the clinics do the surrendering.
Fenn's trade was the counter-discovery.
You could not out-gun a checkpoint.
You could out-*tick* it.
He was twenty-six, wire-thin, superstitious as a relay witch, flying the best boat he would ever fly — not his, which brings this page to Mercer.
The debt was four years old now and had not aged a day; Mercer's debts were like that, embalmed at signing.

Fenn's first boat — his own, paid for, named for his mother — had been impounded at a weighing station in the first year of the weighing stations with a hold full of vaccine stock that sat, at temperature, under seal, for nine days while the paperwork established what everyone already knew, and the stock died, and the fine for *attempted unlicensed distribution of controlled medical materiel* ate the boat.

The vaccines had been bound for a fever town on Cydon's southern terraces, which got its medicine a month late off a subsidized Synod relief barge, newsreader attached, and that was the quarter Fenn stopped believing the war had sides in any shape his cargo could recognize.
Mercer found him on the Ranger Command dock with his license in one hand and nothing in the other, looked at him the way he looked at manifests, and said: "You'll fly a boat of mine.
Fast one.
You'll run what you run — I don't ask, so I can't sell the answer — and someday, when it matters, I'll ask you for a thing, and you'll do it, and we're square."
No rate.
No schedule.
Fenn, who had just learned exactly what paper was worth, took the handshake, and had flown four years since with the debt riding co-pilot, weightless and unexpiring.
Twice a year a crate came aboard with trader routing and no questions allowed — Mercer calling a gram of the interest — and Fenn flew it blind and told his rules to shut up.

He knew three smugglers who owed Mercer likewise; none had ever been asked for the thing; all flew a little crooked under the waiting, which, Fenn suspected, was the actual interest: Mercer collected in *readiness*.
The Vantor run he flew free, and it was the only cargo he never once flew crooked.

The burn ward had a supply officer now, insofar as a free clinic bleeding into a war had anything so grand: the medic with the scarred palm and the two-column book, who audited his deliveries on the pad, ampoule by ampoule, against her own manifest.
The first time he had bristled — smugglers do not enjoy audits — and she had looked up at him with those unbudgeting eyes and said: "The Synod weighs this cargo to starve people.
I weigh it to dose them.
Same scale, opposite religion.
You want to fly for my column, you fly audited.
Nothing vague leaves this pad.
Vague kills burn patients three weeks out, when the culture count comes up short."
"And what's the pay for flying audited?"
Fenn asked, mostly to have something insolent to say while he fell in line — he had been in line since the ninth day of those nine, the ones his first cargo spent dying under seal.
Mira had turned the book around and shown him a page: SAVED, and a column of entries, and at the bottom, new ink, *supply line, one boat, fast.*
"You're in it," she said.
"That's the whole wage.
Ask the lane what it trades at."
It traded high.

He flew the cold chain every quarter after — dermal packs, cultured graft stock, the antivirals the checkpoints loved best because scarcity is the tithe's favorite flavor — at clinic speed, no detours, hold scrubbed to surgery standard, and he never told Mira his rules and she never told him hers, and between the two sets of rules not one ampoule had gone vague since the ward's first winter.
Ally was the dock's word for what they were, and for once the dock had weighed a thing correctly.
Fenn kept a copy of her manifest taped inside the false floor, so that if the floor was ever lifted, the first thing the lifting hand would find was the truth of what the lie was for.
The floor was lifted at the Ferrus reach checkpoint, on a grey watch, in the fourth winter, by an assessor with a leash in her voice and her own escort watching her.

He knew the boarding was wrong before the tube sealed — wrong flavor: not the bored violence of a picket crew skimming for confiscables, but a night-rolls audit team, ledger people, sent down to weigh a sample of the lane because someone upstairs was checking the checkers.

The assessor came through his hold with a lens and a folio and the fastidious exhaustion of a person who had been correct too long at too high a price, and she found the seam in the false floor in four minutes, which was three faster than the professionals managed, because the professionals were looking for cargo and she was looking for *error*.
She lifted the plate.

She read the cold chain — the packs, the cultures, the ticking clocks — and she read Mira's manifest taped where he'd taped it, the truth under the lie, ampoule counts in a medic's hand with a burn ward's stamp.
Fenn stood very still and did the smuggler's whole liturgy: you do not speak, you do not sweat, you think about furniture.
The assessor was quiet for a long moment.

"Agricultural solvents," she said, in a voice with nothing in it at all, and wrote it, and stamped it — the stamp pressed hard, harder than paper needs, the way you stamp a thing you are holding down — *verified, no yield*, and set the plate back with her own hands, squarer than he'd had it.
Her escort saw an assessor completing an inspection.
Fenn, from one meter, saw what it cost, and read the name on the stamp because names were the one cargo you never left on the deck: SIRIN.
At the tube she paused, not turning, and said, conversational, to the bulkhead: "Your trim is two degrees off with the floor loaded.
That plate sits proud when it's full and a *thorough* inspector reads trim before she reads holds.
Ballast forward of frame nine.
Correct it."

And was gone, leash and folio and escort, an enemy in an iron sash who had just taught him to hide his mercy from her own employers better, which was either treason or the only correctness left standing, and Fenn flew the rest of the run repeating the sentence like a rule, rule three, the one he did not fully understand yet and obeyed anyway: *ballast forward of frame nine.*
He told Mira, on the Vantor pad, because the cold chain ran on true things.

She stopped mid-audit, and something crossed her face that he could not price — later he learned the assessor's name lived in margins all up and down the lane, deferred-us margins, a whole scattered bookkeeping of people she had failed to save and saved anyway — and Mira said, quietly, "Add her to your rules," and went back to counting ampoules.
He did.

Rule four, the last rule, the whole trade in one line, taped inside the false floor under the manifest where the truth lived: *Somewhere inside their arithmetic there are people still doing sums by hand.
Fly so they can afford you.*
The clocks ticked.
The boat ran cold and quiet, two degrees truer.
Medicine wants to move, and that winter — audited by one side, absolved by a stamp on the other, indebted to a man who collected in readiness — it moved.
The ring chapel at Halcyon had no roof now, which Lumen considered an improvement the Synod had not intended to make.
A chapel is a hull for the word; holed, it vents the word straight up the well, and the congregation could stand in the gutted nave and watch their amens leave.
He had re-consecrated the ruin himself under the only writ he trusted: use.
A place is sacred that people keep coming to when nothing requires them to.
By that writ the chapel had never been holier.
Lumen's parish was not the station.
It was the lanes.

He rode the convoys the way other clergy kept a district — out with the grain, back with the wounded, third boat of any formation, the seat the superstitious avoid — and his office was the office of the names.

When a hull went silent, the fleets logged tonnage, the tribunals attribution, Wren up the spur pressed its last hum into her book of ghosts; and then, at dusk, Lumen did the part the war had no column for.
He said the names, slowly, in order of watch, and rang the small hand bell after each — the lane keeps the body; we keep the name — and let the amens go up.
He had kept it every dusk without one omission, and the lane, which believes nothing officially and everything at third watch, quietly rebuilt its schedules around it.
Attendance took itself.
The dusk office he had from Crux, and Crux he had from the bell.

She had come to the chapel when the war was young, before the burning — a Warden elder with a cockpit bell bolted over her canopy, sister-cast to the dock bell itself — and had stood at the back of his office, lips moving half a beat ahead of his, reading her own list, the long one, begun at the Sundering.
Afterward they compared liturgies like two old technicians comparing tools.
She taught him that a list is spoken in the order the dead fell, never alphabetical — order of watch is order of debt.
He taught her that a name said angry goes to the wrong address, a lesson his own grief had priced.

She called his chapel a decent hangar for the practice; he called her list the only complete archive in the lanes; and they were friends from that dusk on, two keepers of one flame on different shifts — arguing about everything except the thing, and about the thing, never.
When the Ember Vigil burned and Crux's band went silent, Lumen said her dead for her, every dusk, half a year, and did not add her name to any list: you do not name a thing you do not want to arrive.

She came back with the spring convoys, grounded, greyer, her boat in tow like a led animal, and stood at the back of the office with her lips moving, and Lumen finished the names and rang the bell and did not turn until the amens were up: you let a returned ghost decide when she is seen.

Then she came up the aisle and put a folded paper on the altar-slab — her list, the whole list, the Sundering to the Vigil, Aldan at its head in letters gone soft with re-inking — and said, "In case my mouth stops before the list does.
You're the other bell, Lumen.
When one cracks, the sister carries the toll."
That is all the war ever got from Crux about the Vigil fire; it was addressed to a friend; this book records it as Aurelia would: dated, attributed, improved not at all.
Ember he did not get from anyone.
Ember he was given, the way wards are given: by sitting still.

He had ridden a medicine convoy into Vantor in the burn ward's first winter and stayed — chaplains stay where the grief is densest — and in the mask-months, when the stoker girl could not yet speak and could not stop hearing, he read her the convoy schedules aloud, like psalms: a burned spacer does not want heaven described; she wants proof the lanes still run.

When the mask came off and the voice came back a grate, there was a name riding in it — the one whose scales had fired on her boat, asked for at a medic's bedside, repeated after dusk like a fever count — and the ward went carefully deaf, and Lumen leaned in with the one piece of doctrine he was certain of: "Not in the dark, stoker.
A name said in the dark is a prayer to the wrong god, and that one answers.
You'll keep the name — I've carried mine since before you could walk a deck — but you'll keep it *banked*.
Say it in daylight, aloud, once, where the sky can audit you, and never feed it after dusk.
That's the whole rule.
The coal stays covered.
Covered coal boils water; open coal burns the house."

She glared at him out of the unburned eye, one-third of a face doing a whole argument, and kept the rule the way she kept feed-counts, exactly; wards choose themselves, and choose you, and the paperwork is a look across a burn ward and a rule accepted.

Now she flew the clinic runs through Halcyon, and every landfall she stood the dusk office and took the hand bell for the stoker names — her right, her boat's four — and afterward, in daylight only, made her single banked withdrawal at altitude's edge where the sky could audit her.
Lumen counted that ledger more carefully than any chaplain admits.
The day she said the name twice, the coal was out of the bank.
It had not happened.
She was the best-kept fire on the lanes, and he would put his hand in it for her.
Which brings this page, in the order of debt, to the citations.
They arrived with the seized crews — Vargo's crews, put off in boats with water, rations, and a signed judgment, every forfeiture attributed, every ruin receipted.

The boats drifted in to Halcyon, as everything did, and the crews came up the well — hulls gone, livelihoods adjudicated, four generations of a bunker family converted into an exhibit — holding the judgments the way the bereaved hold anything official: as if it might yet be exchanged for what it cost.
Lumen read the first one at dusk, after the names, in his office voice, and then, before the nave, he buried it.
Full rites.
The little spade from the garden kit; a grave the size of a folio page, cut into the slag where the altar's shadow fell; the citation laid in seals up; the bell rung once, as for a name.
"Here lies the law," Lumen said over it, "that a good man fed to a warlord because we would not keep it ourselves.
It was correct.
It saved no one.
We commit it to the ground it measured.
Let it rise in a better jurisdiction."
And the seized crews wept, which the judge would have noted was not evidence, and which was the entire point: somebody in the lanes had finally issued them a document that matched their losses.

He buried every citation brought to him, at dusk, with rites, in rows, and the rows grew offerings — washers, ration tabs, a prayer coin stamped side down — and the lane named the plot *Vargo's acre*, with the tenderness reserved for perfect enemies.

Word traveled up the spur and across the line that a chaplain at Halcyon held funerals for the tribunal's paperwork; and Lumen, who hated no living man without paying for it in vigils, examined his conscience regarding the attribution man, and found the hatred sound, and kept it.
Not for the seizures; fleets seize.
For the receipts.

For the patient conversion of every mercy in the lanes into an exhibit — the salvage logs subpoenaed, the deferrals annotated, the drowned billed for the water — a bookkeeping so perfect it needed only one omission to become monstrous: any column anywhere for what things cost the living.
Vargo kept excellent records.
Lumen kept graves for them.
Each thought the other's office the blasphemy; only one rang a bell over it; and the bell, as bells do, was winning.
At dusk, the office.
The names in order of watch.

Crux's list in the reliquary with Aldan at its head; the folded citations under the slag in their patient rows; Ember's coal banked and audited; the hand bell's small clear note going up the well after each name like a soul with good trim.
*The lane keeps the body.
We keep the name.
While one keeper is breathing, the office is kept.*
Amens going up the well.
Lamps coming on across the ruin.
The office, kept.

Vega came back to Halcyon in the fourth year, flying escort on a grain convoy, and counted hulls on the approach the way her mother had taught her to count storms — and there it was, the old arithmetic of Dock Nine, waiting in the ruin like a habit the burning had failed to take: four grain haulers, two tenders, one water boat, her own dart on flank, and no tithe-barge anywhere under heaven with a horizon of its own.
She ran the count twice, the way Crux had taught her to confirm a fix, and both counts agreed: everything in that sky was theirs.
She chalked two stars on the dart before every sortie now.
One for Tam, as promised.

The second she had added the morning after the school burned — not for a person; for a place; for the Ember Vigil, which had taught her the fix and the drift and the honest correction, and which existed now the way her brother did: as a debt she flew with, chalked where her hand could reach.

The Vigil's survivors were scattered up the lanes, unfinished cadets flying finished pilots' legs, carrying the covenant in their mouths; it had been enough at the Sundering; it was having to be again.

Some nights Vega recited the old words to the empty band just to hear the school still had a voice, and somewhere out on the same dark a dozen unfinished voices were doing the same, and that — not the stone annex, not the burned hangars — was the Vigil now.
You could not tithe it.
It had no yield.
It was all margin.

Her teacher was back — the spring's one grace: Crux, grounded, greyer, silent about the fire like a door closed on a room you would be shown eventually or never, standing dusk offices at the ring chapel with her long list.
Vega had somewhere to send her dead again.

She gave her four Vigil names the first evening, watched them folded into the order of watch without a dropped syllable, and understood what the woman had meant on Cydon: the ledger passes uphill when it must, comes back down when you can carry it, and the carrying is the covenant.
Hale had her report to the desk by the bell, every landfall — stationmaster's standing order, four years running.

He was older each time in the way load-bearing things age, and he audited her escort logs personally, which he did for no other pilot; she flew them knowing it, which made her exact, which was the entire mercy of it.
This landfall he read her log, grunted at the fuel margins, and took a flat parcel from the desk's one locked drawer.
"Found it in the salvage sort three quarters ago," he said.
"Been deciding whether giving it back was a kindness.
I've decided it's a debt."
It was a page.
Scorched down one edge, cut clean along the other — cut, not torn; he had gone into a burned maintenance log with a blade and taken the margin out whole.
Her margin.
The one the auditors never read because margins had no yield: *Brann, reassigned.
Sirin, deferred us.
Kaine.*
Her own handwriting, four years younger, pressing hard enough to survive.
"The station's gone to the past tense," Hale said while she held it.
"The books burned, the manifests burned, the office they were correct in burned.
What the fire couldn't collect was the margins.
First two so you'd remember."
He put his cap on, the work signal, and the heavy hand arrived on her shoulder the way it had over a grey harbor on tithe day.
"I've watched you fly four years on the third name, girl.
Good fuel.
Burns steady.
But you wrote it down *so you would never need to* — so the hating could live on the page and the pilot could fly the boat.
Keep it that way.
The ones who promote that name off the page into the cockpit — I've flown with them.
They brake late at everything forever, and one day the everything is a docking ring, or a wingman.
You're my ward, which was never in writing either.
Consider both entries audited."
"Yes, stationmaster."
And then, because the desk was the one place in the lanes where true things went one for one: "It's not hate keeping me exact anymore.
It's escort.
I have people behind me now."
"I know," Hale said.
"That's the graduation, Vega.
Nobody holds a ceremony because there's a war on.
Go see your cousin; she came in on the grain."
Kestrel was fifteen now and had a broker's way of entering a dock, reading the whole ring before her boots cleared the ramp.

They went up to the loft together, neither proposing it, both steering — her father's radio shack over the Dock Nine gantries, built out of manifest errors, sealed once by the Synod and unsealed by the simple method of the Synod losing the station.
The burning had taken the gantry crane, the dorm spur, the manifest office and every correct page in it.
The loft it had missed.
Of course it had.
The loft had never been on an inventory in its life.
"They got the yield," Kestrel said, palming the set warm, her old chair creaking under her like a password.
"Missed the margin.
Again."
Then, without ceremony, because the family tariff was true things one for one: "Juno-6's net came through.
A berth number, Vega.
Not a yard roll — a *berth*.
Hull maintenance sub-tier, inside the line, both names on one watch rotation.
Alive.
Working.
Together.
Still."
She said the fourth word the way other people say amen.
"I read the sixty last night, eighth-night regular, and afterward keyed the two of them a message on the old frequency.
First time.
I didn't say *I'm coming* — you don't bet what you can't laugh off.
I said *the band is kept*.
If it reaches them, they'll know whose fist that is."
"When the lane opens that yard," Vega said — not *if*; the cousins had never once traded in *if* — "I fly the boat that goes and gets them.
That's posted.
Put it in the margin."
Kestrel wrote it on the slate, small and hard, under *Sable, who checked* and *Together* and *Vega found her door*: *Vega flies the boat.*
Margin law: what is written there has no yield, and the war has never once collected it.

Then they sat with the set warm and the whip aimed at the outbound corridor, two women of one family standing the watch their elders had invented chair by chair, the ruin humming its stubborn note below, and it was, for one eighth-night, nearly peace.

The war, having excellent hearing, answered inside the week: Synod survey skiffs walking the Halcyon approaches in long patient rows — mapping flights, said a language that no longer had diplomats — counting berths, counting sockets, counting the ruin the way you count a thing you intend to own.
Hale read the flimsy with his cap off and said the quiet part in his flat stationmaster's voice: "They burned it to teach us it was worthless.
Now they've done the sums on what it costs them open.
Next time they don't come to burn, girl.
They come to *keep*."
Vega stood at the dead bell with the scorched margin folded in her jacket over the sternum and made the only oath of her life she made out loud, alone, to a bell that could not ring.

They would lose this place — she was a navigator; she could read a mass-curve as well as Thorne — and losing it would not end the arithmetic, because the station had taught the lane its lesson twice now: you can burn the yield; the margins keep.
"We'll be back for the bell," she told it.
"And you'll ring."

She was right, twice over, and the price of it was years, and this book gets there — through the schism lanes and the long burn, to a morning when that cracked bronze speaks over Dock Nine again and the sound goes down the lane like water coming in at the top.
It is coming.
Everything in the lanes gets there.

The convoy went out at dawn, grain empty, wounded loaded, Vega on flank with two chalk stars, three names, one posted promise, flying exact — not for the third name in the margin but for the first two, for the sixty, for the two on one watch rotation, alive, working, together, still — for the whole cargo of the other column, which the fleets have never managed to count, and which this book was written to keep.
So it would never need to be remembered.
So it could never be forgot.
